title: A Cross Burning and the Mill Lawsuits
summary: Fletcher Anderson recalls the Klan cross burning that got Bogalusa's attention and the rigidly segregated Crown Zellerbach paper mill where he hired on in 1962, where he and Robert Hicks filed lawsuits that opened jobs, supervisor posts, and employment for Black women that had never existed before.

title: Marching for Jobs and a Voice
summary: He describes carrying the fight from the plant into a city that hired no Black workers and seated no Black officials, the many marches to the courthouse, to Franklinton, and to Baton Rouge, the federal injunction against police brutality, naming the first Black policemen, and the boycotts that finally opened employment.

title: Finding the Hicks Family
summary: Cynthia Anderson recounts the cross burning drawing her in, and Fletcher coming home to say he wanted to meet the man he was about to die for, Robert Hicks, leading them to the Hicks home where, after she handed over her driver's license, the families became a team.

title: Fletcher's Bogalusa Roots
summary: Fletcher traces his grandparents George and Maggie Anderson, who came from Mississippi for the paper mill, his grandfather a taxi driver and one of the first Black men to register and vote, his grandmother a boarding-house keeper, both college-educated property owners, and his own marriage and three children.

title: Cynthia, Raised a Hunter
summary: Cynthia describes growing up in the middle quarters among the poorer Black families as outdoors people who hunted and fished, the basketball her mother let her play as long as she made the honor roll, and the hurt of learning how people felt about her that made her want to fight back at desegregation.

title: Working Up the Mill Line
summary: Fletcher recounts climbing the mill's job line from loader to trucker to slitter operator to overhead crane operator, having to train himself on jobs white workers held, the dangerous crane work that injured him into retirement, and the strange tension of picketing the company he then had to clock in for.

title: Protecting the Community
summary: Fletcher describes the Deacons protecting the Meredith March and their own neighborhoods with walkie-talkies, block captains, and the ability to block streets against drive-by shootings, since the police were there to protect the Klan, and how the model came from a charter meeting with the Jonesboro deacons.

title: Hunters, Not Aggressors
summary: He explains that the men were lifelong hunters who knew weapons but had never shot at a person, raised by elders to stay out of trouble, and recounts being attacked leaving a cafe with three coworkers and then charged, absurdly, with the attempted murder of the restaurant's waitresses.

title: A Wife Who Believed in Self-Defense
summary: Cynthia recounts teaching in Covington, coming home to her husband's swollen head and a beaten car, climbing onto the car to wrench open the fire-station door to retrieve it, Mrs. Hicks calming her fury, and answering the warnings against the Deacons with her conviction that if you do not defend yourself, no one will.

title: A.Z. Young, Bob Hicks, and Gayle Jenkins
summary: Fletcher reflects on the fear in a Black community dependent on white employers, and the leaders who pressed on, A.Z. Young taking over the Voters League, Robert Hicks, and Gayle Jenkins, who came to the school board through appointment after Edwin Edwards was elected governor.

title: Appointed, Then Redistricted
summary: He describes how the first Black officials held appointed seats until lawsuits forced redistricting that let the Black community elect Gayle Jenkins, R.T. Young, and the first city councilmen, and the districting trick that split neighbors on the same street into different districts to dilute their power.

part: Strong Women, and the Deacon Ranks
title: A Movement Where Women Had a Voice
summary: Cynthia describes Bogalusa as a rare community where Black women had a voice and men turned to ask their wives' judgment, and remembers Gayle Jenkins as a fireball, and the women who desegregated the vocational college, kept enrolled four years without being allowed to graduate, and became nurses anyway.

title: Charles Sims and the Deacon Leadership
summary: Fletcher recounts the leadership elected from that first meeting, Charles Sims as president, Robert Hicks as vice president, himself as lieutenant, Sims a fiery, intimidating man who could rouse a crowd, and the key members like Sam Barnes, Reese Perkins, and Willie Jane Preston.

title: The Informant, and Arming Themselves
summary: He reflects on the informant whose presence put the Deacons' names on the governor's desk within days, the difficulty of voicing such suspicions, and how they armed themselves with carbines, thirty-thirties, and handguns bought with donations from people like Louis Lomax, trained by veterans and by secret helpers too afraid to be named.

title: Determined Through the Harassment
summary: Fletcher describes the summer of 1965 at its height, the endless marching while still holding a job, the police harassment on the highways with tickets that cost them their insurance, and a determination not to be stopped that won black state troopers and opened doors in the surrounding towns.

title: A Klansman Shot in Self-Defense
summary: He recounts the march when a Klansman attacked the marchers and had to be shot in self-defense, the attempted-murder charges thrown out by the lawyers Lolis Elie and Robert Collins, and Cynthia's own arrest, jailed for refusing to say sir to the trooper who pulled her over behind a school bus.

title: Protecting CORE and the Workers
summary: Fletcher describes the Deacons protecting James Farmer and the CORE workers, Canadian Griffith assigned to escort them, and the violence that went unpunished, Griffith beaten on Columbia Street and a soldier home from the military, Captain Seymour, shot in a phone booth while the mayor never came.

title: The March to Baton Rouge and the Klan Exposed
summary: He recalls CORE and Dick Gregory's economic message, the march to Baton Rouge with its standoff in a Hammond park where they circled the cars like wagons and built a latrine truck, the Klan thick along the highway, and how, with the Klan exposed, he watched his own supervisor collect money for it at the plant.

title: What Integration Did to Education
summary: Cynthia argues that for all the surface change, Bogalusa let its public spaces die rather than integrate, that white flight to private schools followed, and that integration hurt education when teachers who would not correct Black children let them drift into special education.

title: Education Is the Key
summary: The Andersons reflect that they would change the world to see a person as a person rather than a color, doubt racism will ever end, and hold that education is the key to ending it, since the less educated are where the Klan finds its recruits and an educated person will not drag another behind a car.

title: Reaching the Young
summary: Asked about young people who have not taken up the ballot, they point to drugs they believe were planted in the community and to teen pregnancy and poverty that trap a generation, and to the successful who will not reach back, insisting that a life is not worth living if you have not made it better for someone else.

title: The Deacons Took the Fear Away
summary: They close on whether Bogalusa's movement could have happened without self-defense, and say it could not: the Deacons let people sleep knowing someone would protect them, took the fear out of the Klan by reminding them the same bullet kills anyone, and helped hold down the violence in a city that, even in 2011, was only just merging its one Black and one white YMCA.
